title: Grape Consumption and UV Resistance in Humans. The first human study that established the effect of oral administration of dietary table grape on ultraviolet treatment was reported by Elmets and coworkers. The study evaluates the effects of dietary table grape consumption on ultraviolet-induced skin damage in 19 healthy humans over the course of 2 weeks. They reported an overall protective effect of dietary grapes, suggesting greater resistance to ultraviolet-induced sunburn. When analyzing the response of the 19 subjects individually, the minimal erythema dose, a measure of ultraviolet resistance which was determined before and after treatment, increased in 11 participants, remained unchanged in 7, and decreased in only 1.

sentences.text: Grape Consumption and UV Resistance in Humans.
The first human study that established the effect of oral administration of dietary table grape on ultraviolet treatment was reported by Elmets and coworkers.
The study evaluates the effects of dietary table grape consumption on ultraviolet-induced skin damage in 19 healthy humans over the course of 2 weeks.
They reported an overall protective effect of dietary grapes, suggesting greater resistance to ultraviolet-induced sunburn.

When analyzing the response of the 19 subjects individually, the minimal erythema dose, a measure of ultraviolet resistance which was determined before and after treatment, increased in 11 participants, remained unchanged in 7, and decreased in only 1.
The authors noted that the photoprotective effect is not attributed to changes in cutaneous pigmentation, as no change in melanin index was observed before and after treatment.

Because polyphenols are considered key contributors to the photoprotective effects of grapes, the dietary table grape preparation was analyzed by mass spectrometry to identify additional active constituents.

The analysis revealed that dietary table grape contains polyphenols, including catechins, anthocyanidins, isorhamnetin, and resveratrol, all of which are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

Overall, the studies demonstrate that grape consumption enhances ultraviolet protection in a subset of individuals without significant adverse effects, highlighting the potential of dietary polyphenols in reducing photodamage and promoting skin health.

The study reported by Pezzuto and coworkers was designed to explore the systemic photoprotective effects of dietary grape consumption, with an objective of independently confirming and expanding the results of Elmets and coworkers.

In addition to baseline minimal erythema dose and chromameter measurements to assess ultraviolet-induced skin response, this study included fecal sample analysis, urine analysis, and whole blood and plasma analysis, as well as the collection of skin biopsies.

Consistent with the findings of Elmets and colleagues, this study with 29 human participants confirms that consuming grapes for two weeks reduces skin sensitivity to ultraviolet radiation in a significant portion of the study population.

Further, Pezzuto and coworkers conducted a detailed analysis, where individual subjects were grouped, for example, if a positive response was observed, the study aimed to explore what made the responsive individuals unique.
Moreover, those who showed greater ultraviolet resistance could be clearly distinguished from the rest of the group.
The study focused on analyzing the gut-skin axis, which refers to the connection between gut health, skin health, and the role of the microbiome.

Specifically, it examined the microbiome composition and the impact of grape consumption on metabolite profiles, including a K E G G pathway analysis to identify biological processes influenced by these changes.

While the variety of microbial species within a single person's gut microbiome and compared among groups appeared unaltered, striking differences in the microbiome and metabolome were resolved when comparing the individuals showing greater ultraviolet resistance with nonresponders.
Further, the authors showed that grape consumption modulated microbiota abundance, enzyme levels, and K E G G pathways.
Importantly, three urine metabolites, 2'-deoxyribonic acid, 3-hydroxyphenylacetic acid, and scyllo-inositol, were lower in the ultraviolet-resistant group.

Notably, measurements of 2'-deoxyribonic acid, a byproduct of oxidative D N A damage, could identify a ultraviolet skin nonresponder as a predictor of ultraviolet sensitivity with 71.8 percent accuracy.
Differences in ultraviolet sensitivity might also be linked to genetic variations in the glutathione S-transferase enzyme, which helps protect against oxidative stress.
The study concluded that some people could develop greater resistance to ultraviolet-induced skin damage by eating grapes, possibly due to changes in the gut-skin axis.
While it is not certain if the gut-skin axis directly causes this ultraviolet resistance, there is a clear correlation.
More broadly, these findings suggest that grapes might influence other health benefits through similar mechanisms.
